section.title: Do Not Harden Your Hearts
section.sectionRationale: The second warning passage opens by quoting Psalm 95, holding up the wilderness generation as a solemn example. They heard God's voice, hardened their hearts, and were barred from the promised rest. The writer applies this 'today' to his readers, urging daily mutual encouragement lest anyone be hardened by sin's deceit and fall through unbelief.
section.thesis: The wilderness generation heard God's voice yet hardened their hearts in unbelief and so were shut out of God's rest; therefore, as long as it is called today, believers must guard against a hardened, unbelieving heart and exhort one another daily to hold their first assurance firm to the end.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the quotation of Psalm 95 warning against hardening as in the rebellion; then the writer's application, calling for daily mutual encouragement and probing the wilderness example to show that unbelief kept them from the rest.

units.label: The Spirit's warning from Psalm 95
units.note: today, if you hear His voice

verses.text: Therefore, as the Holy Spirit says: “{{semeron|Today}}, if you hear His voice,
do not {{skleryno|harden}} your hearts, as you did in the rebellion, in the day of testing in the wilderness,
where your fathers tested and tried Me, and for forty years saw My works.
Therefore I was angry with that generation, and I said, ‘Their hearts are always going astray, and they have not known My ways.’
So I swore on oath in My anger, ‘They shall never enter My {{katapausis|rest}}.’”
See to it, brothers, that none of you has a wicked heart of {{apistia|unbelief}} that turns away from the living God.
But exhort one another daily, as long as it is called today, so that none of you may be hardened by sin’s deceitfulness.
We have come to share in Christ if we hold firmly to the end the assurance we had at first.
As it has been said: “Today, if you hear His voice, do not harden your hearts, as you did in the rebellion.”
For who were the ones who heard and rebelled? Were they not all those Moses led out of Egypt?
And with whom was God angry for forty years? Was it not with those who sinned, whose bodies fell in the wilderness?
And to whom did He swear that they would never enter His rest? Was it not to those who disobeyed?
So we see that it was because of their unbelief that they were unable to enter.

semeron.gloss: Today, now. Psalm 95's 'today' becomes the writer's watchword: the window of opportunity to respond to God's voice is the present moment, an open door that will not always stand open.

skleryno.gloss: To harden, make stubborn or unyielding. The heart can be hardened by refusing God's voice and by the deceit of sin; the wilderness generation shows where a hardened heart leads, to exclusion from God's rest.

apistia.gloss: Unbelief, faithlessness. The root sin of the wilderness generation was not merely misbehaviour but a heart of unbelief that turned away from the living God; unbelief, the writer concludes, is why they could not enter the rest.

katapausis.gloss: Rest, a settled place of repose. In Psalm 95 it is the promised land the rebels forfeited; the writer will develop it into the deeper, ultimate rest of God that still stands open to those who believe.

basic.context: The writer quotes Psalm 95, which looks back to Israel in the wilderness. That generation was rescued from Egypt, saw God's works for forty years, yet hardened their hearts, rebelled, and were barred from the promised land. The writer applies this warning to his readers: today, while there is still time, do not harden your hearts. Encourage one another daily so no one falls through unbelief.
basic.hermeneutics: Notice the writer treats Psalm 95 as the Holy Spirit still speaking 'today'. Scripture addresses the present reader. Read the wilderness generation as a mirror and a warning, not merely ancient history.
basic.originalLanguages: The refrain is sēmeron, today, the open moment. The danger is a heart that is sklērynō, hardened, through the deceit of sin. The root problem is apistia, unbelief. And the loss is exclusion from God's katapausis, rest.
basic.typology: The wilderness generation and the promised land are a type. Their rescue from Egypt yet failure to enter the land pictures the peril of professing faith yet falling short of the rest God has prepared, which the next passage identifies as a deeper rest fulfilled in Christ.
basic.christ: To share in Christ is the great privilege at stake; the warning guards it. The voice not to be resisted 'today' is finally the voice of God in the Son, and holding firm to the end is holding firm to Him.
basic.god: God is the living God who speaks, whom unbelief turns away from. He is patient over forty years yet also just, swearing that the unbelieving would not enter His rest. His warnings are part of His care.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 95 is the source text. Numbers 14 records the rebellion at Kadesh. 1 Corinthians 10 holds up the wilderness generation as a warning to us.
basic.oneStory: Israel's failure in the wilderness stands as a signpost in the biblical story: those who hear God's voice must respond in faith, today, or forfeit the rest He promises, a rest that finds its true home in Christ.

context.literary: The passage begins the long central warning, quoting Psalm 95:7-11 and then expounding it with a series of rhetorical questions in verses 16 to 18. The keyword 'today' links the ancient psalm to the present readers, and 'rest' becomes the bridge into chapter 4.
context.historical: The exodus generation had been redeemed from Egypt but repeatedly tested God, culminating in the refusal to enter Canaan at Kadesh-barnea, so that they died in the wilderness. The readers, redeemed by Christ, stand at a similar crossroads of faith or hardening.
context.canonical: The passage joins the wider biblical use of the wilderness as a cautionary example and reads the Psalms as the voice of the Spirit addressing every generation, undergirding the Christian conviction that Scripture speaks a living word 'today'.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To warn the readers, by the example of the wilderness generation, against a hardened and unbelieving heart, and to summon them to daily mutual encouragement so that they hold their assurance firm to the end.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The wilderness narrative is described as a warning; the prescriptions are explicit: see to it that no one has an unbelieving heart, and exhort one another daily. The abiding command is vigilant, communal perseverance.

debates.question: Were the wilderness generation ever truly God's redeemed people, and can believers likewise fall?

views.label: A warning that genuine faith perseveres
views.case: Those who hardened their hearts showed their unbelief; true faith holds firm to the end, and the passage teaches that only persevering faith proves one has come to share in Christ.

views.label: A real possibility to be guarded against
views.case: The warning is addressed to the church as a real summons; the deceitfulness of sin can harden any heart, and the command to watch and exhort daily assumes the danger is genuine.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge on the same response: heed the warning today, guard your heart against unbelief, and keep encouraging one another, for endurance marks those who truly share in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The passage pulses with a single adverb, sēmeron, today, the ever present moment of decision quoted from Psalm 95. Against it stands the verb sklērynō, to harden, describing what happens to a heart that refuses God's voice and is duped by the deceitfulness of sin. Beneath the hardening lies apistia, unbelief, named in verse 12 as a wicked heart that turns away from the living God and in verse 19 as the very reason the wilderness generation could not enter. What they forfeited was katapausis, rest, the settled inheritance God had sworn to give, a word the writer will deepen in the next section into the rest of God Himself.

typology.type: The exodus generation redeemed yet unbelieving
typology.fulfillment: A warning type of those who profess faith yet fall short through unbelief, fulfilled as a caution to the church.
A shadow of the deeper rest of God, entered by faith in Christ, developed in Hebrews 4.

typology.type: Moses leading Israel out of Egypt
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadowing Christ who leads His people to a lasting rest that Joshua's conquest could not finally secure.

study.christ: Though this passage focuses on warning, Christ stands at its centre: verse 14 says we have come to share in Christ if we hold firm to the end. The voice of God that must be heeded 'today' is now heard fully in the Son, and the rest the wilderness generation forfeited is the rest secured by Christ, into which faith in Him alone brings us. To harden the heart against God's voice is, in the end, to turn away from the living God who speaks in His Son.
study.god: God is the living God, personal and speaking, from whom unbelief turns away. He is patient, bearing with a rebellious people and showing them His works for forty years, yet He is also holy and just, roused to righteous anger by persistent unbelief and swearing that the hardened would not enter His rest. His warnings, spoken by the Spirit through Scripture, are the tender severity of a God who wants His people to enter the rest He has prepared.

crossReferences.note: The source text quoted, warning against hardening as at Meribah and Massah.
The rebellion at Kadesh where God swore the generation would not see the land.
The wilderness events written as warnings for us; let anyone who thinks he stands take heed.
The promised rest still stands open, entered by faith, developing the theme of this passage.

study.oneStory: The exodus generation was rescued from Egypt and stood on the edge of the promised rest, yet they hardened their hearts in unbelief and died in the wilderness. Their story is set as a warning in the middle of Scripture: God's rest is entered only by faith that keeps hearing His voice today. The whole story presses toward the true and final rest secured in Christ, and calls every hearer to respond in faith while today remains.

quiz.q: What was the root failure of the wilderness generation?

options.text: A hardened heart of unbelief that turned away from the living God.
options.note: Verses 12 and 19 name unbelief as the root, not mere mistakes.

options.text: They simply lacked information about God.
options.note: They saw God's works for forty years; the problem was unbelief, not ignorance.

options.text: They never left Egypt at all.
options.note: They were led out by Moses but hardened their hearts afterward.

options.text: They worked too hard to please God.
options.note: Their problem was unbelief and rebellion, not excessive effort.

quiz.q: How does the writer treat the words of Psalm 95?

options.text: As the Holy Spirit still speaking 'today' to the readers.
options.note: Verse 7 introduces the quotation as what the Holy Spirit says.

options.text: As an outdated text with no present force.
options.note: He applies it directly and urgently to the present.

options.text: As a private opinion of the psalmist.
options.note: He treats it as the Spirit's living word, not mere human opinion.

options.text: As a prophecy already fully fulfilled and closed.
options.note: Its 'today' remains open and addresses the readers now.

quiz.q: What practical remedy does the writer give against hardening?

options.text: Exhort one another daily, as long as it is called today.
Withdraw and rely only on yourself.
options.note: The remedy is mutual, not isolated.
The call is active daily encouragement, not passive waiting.

options.text: Add more sacrifices to atone in advance.
options.note: The remedy is encouragement and faith, not extra ritual.

quiz.q: According to verse 14, how do we show we have come to share in Christ?

options.text: By holding firmly to the end the assurance we had at first.
options.note: Perseverance in assurance evidences genuine sharing in Christ.

options.text: By a single burst of enthusiasm early on.
options.note: Initial enthusiasm must be held firmly to the end.
It is holding fast, not the absence of all struggle, that marks belonging.
Sharing in Christ is shown by persevering faith, not ancestry.

questions: Hearts harden slowly, often through the small deceits of sin. Where do you sense the beginnings of hardness in your own heart?
The word is 'today'. Is there something God has been saying to you that you keep putting off?
God's remedy is daily encouragement from others. Who encourages your faith, and whose faith are you helping to guard?
The wilderness generation saw God's works yet did not trust Him. Where have you seen God act, and yet still struggle to believe?
Sharing in Christ is shown by holding firm to the end. What would help you keep your first assurance alive over the long haul?

translationNotes.note: 'The rebellion' and 'the day of testing' render the place names Meribah and Massah in Psalm 95, where Israel quarrelled with God over water; the Greek follows the Septuagint in translating rather than transliterating them.
The oath formula 'They shall never enter My rest' is a strong Hebrew idiom of negation carried into Greek, expressing God's solemn refusal.
'Sin's deceitfulness' personifies sin as a deceiver; the daily encouragement of the community is set as the counter to sin's power to harden unnoticed.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hebrews 3:7-19. No em dashes occurred in this passage. This passage begins the second warning section (3:7-4:13); orthodox views on perseverance and apostasy are represented fairly. Sermon set to none; no expositor series confirmed this session.
